speaker: interviewer
text: Alice, will you introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I am proud.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you going to introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would be very happy. Here is my oldest granddaughter, Shelly. I don't know if she's a second or third generation. Her father was six month old when they [INAUDIBLE]. So I don't know. But anyway, she is my great friend and was always very helpful at any situation, especially when my husband passed away when I felt her warm hand on my shoulder. That made my suffering so much easier. And I saw a future in her. And I hope that she will continue to be the same way. And, perhaps, one day she will show this to her children and grandchildren. And that will be the continuation. Her father is not here. And here is my younger son Peter. He's my very good friend. We can carry long conversation. He has a lot of understanding for every situation in life, for the past and the present. And it's a big security for me to have him. Peter has one little daughter. Her name is Allison. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Shelly has a little brother and sister, Ryan and Kimberly. And they are all between five and six years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you react to your grandma's comments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I feel very special. I feel very close with her. And she's a good grandma. She's very good to me. She's very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about that fact that she wants you to hand this down?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel very special. I'm very proud of her.

speaker: interviewee
text: We want to bridge, we want to transfer, this to further generations. And she's the only one in my family that could do this, that could go into the third generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same. I've been wanting this opportunity for a long time, to have a record of this and to hear the complete story. So I'm very happy to have this opportunity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we are very happy you could come.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. You know, we tried to protect our children, Peter and his brother Richard. We wanted them to have a fairly happy childhood. But now is the time to tell them the real story. And there could be nothing better than this program, than this film. And I am sure when they will have a chance to see, to visit all the museums-- they didn't have a chance yet-- so they will have the whole picture. And they can go on from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel that your father's tape has never been made?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's unfortunate. I think my mother has gone to great lengths to record, as accurately as she can, his story. And I'm sure he would give her his blessings. But it would've been nice to have had his tape also. Right, Shelly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my grandfather would tell his story. If someone listened, he would tell his story. And he would have really liked to be here and tell the story, too. But I think that she's doing a pretty good job herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about the Holocaust as you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, I went to Hebrew school and Sunday school. And any chance they'd get, they'd try and teach me something. So they'd always educate me on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You once went to [INAUDIBLE] center. You were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I took my class there. And she gave them a whole production. And she spoke to them, and then a bat mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh, the bat mitzvah also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I've grown up with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you known about your past, your brother's birth? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I've--

speaker: interviewer
text: --beginning--

speaker: interviewee
text: I've known about it. I don't think I was ever fully aware the extent of-- of the atrocities in the Holocaust. And I think in my older years, I'm becoming much more aware of-- of what really happened. And I think it's to my mother's great credit to be so actively involved now in telling the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the trip to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: About the trip to America. OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had to wait for about four or five years to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to-- to get our quota. It was very limited for Polish Jews, which was used up right immediately after the war. The-- the priority were people freed from the concentration camps. And the later comers had to wait for their turn. So we were forced, of course, to live in Germany for this time. We got our papers ready and invitation to the States in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] June '51, 1951. We came on the ship that was coursing between the United States and Europe and bringing back refugees from the camps to-- to United States. We had already-- a lot of our friends were settled down in New York. And they were preparing for our arrival. They rented a place that we would go immediately after arriving. The trip, of course, was-- was-- was very difficult. I was sick all the time on the ship. And it was [INAUDIBLE] took a week. And but arrival, when we saw the Statue of Liberty and we realized we are reaching a new country, a new life that we were praying for, hoping for, that we survived this terrible war, it was a very, very emotional [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] feeling. And, of course, it was also helpful after going on the ground to-- to have good friends that introduced us to all the technology and the luxury that we didn't have it, didn't know before. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we had a very pleasant stay for a few months in New York getting the first lesson in this, what America is. But we decided to move somewhere else. New York was too difficult a place to-- to live. And I had an uncle and aunt in Los Angeles. And they suggested that maybe we will try to establish ourself right here. And Henry came first. He liked it very much. And he brought us over, me and-- in the meantime, I had another son that you just met before. And we came to California. We did have some money we brought from Germany, where he was able to make some money. So we got a car. And we rented an apartment. We bought secondhand furniture. And he started a little business. His friend gave him an idea of opening a little store with leather boots and then some repairs. I was helping, of course. And practically from the very beginning, we made a very moderate, but independent living. We didn't need any help from anybody, any organization. The children went-- Richard, my older son, went to school. Within a few weeks, he learned language. And a seven-year-old boy, at the same time, he spoke three languages. So that was amazing to me that they got very quickly adjusted. Youngest, Peter, went through nursery school. And I was working with Henry, working days and nights to-- to have ends meet. But we were already comfortable. We were going on vacation. We took trips, because it's a beautiful country. One- one Sunday you could go to the ocean, then you go to the mountains. And everything was very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] easy, easy available. We worked hard. We like the country that offered us the opportunity to do whatever we can to do with our lives and with ourselves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We met a lot of friends here from-- from Poland. That was the whole group that was formed, [? club ?] 1939 of Polish survivors, Polish Jews. It was like an instant family to us in that we had quite a few members in real family. My sister was married and had two daughters. We were four of us. My aunt with her husband and the other aunt that survived in Siberia with her husband. So that was a sizable family which was practically a-- a rarity after-- after the war. So we were fortunate in this direction. And we tried to-- our goal, even when we talk, we get together with friends, forget the-- the-- the war time. It was so painful the-- the-- instinctively. Because we realized later that we were so sore, that the wounds need time to heal before you start to talk about it. So that's what we protected our children. But nevertheless, whenever we got together, it was very difficult to avoid not-- not talking about it. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we got-- we became part-- we became citizen of-- of United States. Our-- we were very lucky that the relation but United States and Israel were so close. So we didn't have to choose loyalty. It was perfectly all right to have this double loyalty, to be a good Jew, to support as much Israel as we could, and to love this country that gave us opportunity to become this, what we became. And the children, of course, that was equally important. Our first saving account was for education for the children. It was-- we were still lucky that it wasn't as expensive as now. They bought-- yes, there were bar mitzvahs here in-- in-- in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we observed the holidays. We were still not too-- we were secular Jews. This is what we carry with us, but being very well aware that we are Jewish and religiously and nationality being very close to Israel, supporting as much as possible the causes of Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And life wasn't very easy. There was a lot of hardship where, unfortunately, we had more than our share of health-- health problems. But just learning the discipline of life during the war, we maintained this, too, and will implant this in-- in our children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went through their difficulties. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They finished. They graduated both UCLA. They are both attorneys now and having their own families. We're-- our-- Peter is divorced. But he has this little girl that he adores and is sharing with his former wife his little girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Richard, besides Shelly, he has two little children, Ryan and Kimberly from his second marriage. But everybody lives peacefully. They are very much devoted to each other. We have close contact. But everybody has their own life. To me personally, what's the worst thing was the loss of my husband. We went through life together. He was my protector for many, many years in America. We were equal partners. We worked together. We were raising the family. We were fighting all the situations that were we had to survive. Towards-- many years prior to his death, he was very sick. And then I took over. I was taking care of him. And that was a full cycle of life. After he passed away, I went to I went to the Martyrs Memorial, to the Federation. I was lost. I wanted to look for some voluntary work. And also, I felt that now is the time to talk about our history and also our being witnesses to other people's history in this terrible period of Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking from perspective now, I can evaluate better. I can understand better some situations-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to have a different pictures of the situation. Why that way and not the other way? For a while, I was a little bit embarrassed that we were not more active. That was the question that our children through at us. Why didn't you fight back? That was a painful question. And we had to think hard how to answer. But you were mostly-- not only us, but generally speaking-- people were deprived of their possessions, deprived of their families, being thrown from one corner to another, one corner of the world to another, being humiliated, being stripped of their clothes, shaved of their hair, printed number on-- tattoo number on your hand. And that was a person. And being in this spirit or close to it, how could you think of fighting? Did you go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: To Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Poland eight years ago. I didn't find anything that was in my memories, in my imagination. I didn't find any friends. I didn't find people. The new people that-- there was a-- there were different cities, different population. Lots of vulgarity, probably existing anti-Semitism still. However, I didn't experience this on my visit. I went to Auschwitz for the first time. And that was a terrible, horrible experience. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was the physical contact with this what my people were-- had to live through or die there. So I-- I was sick for a couple days after the visit to Auschwitz. I didn't find anything in Poland. I went to Israel last year. It's a wonderful country. My people-- I was proud of all the accomplishments of-- of-- all what these people-- giving them all the credit for their patience, for their know-how, for their difficulties, for their spirits that went through, what they built. That I am part of it, not physically. But these are my people. It's my country. It's-- I follow very closely. I came home to America. Here is my country, too, which gave me the room, which gave me the freedom, which gave me the opportunity, to-- to-- the chance to educate the family the way we wanted without questioning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am proud of it. But that wasn't all, still. There was still something missing. At this stage, I am almost well over 70, you know. So it's very close to the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thinking now, who am I? What is my identity? Where I am from? What is really the background? And I find the answer in-- in the museum in Washington. What is your message to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you mean Jewish generations?

speaker: interviewer
text: In general. In general.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or, general generation? What is the message?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, what we unfortunately see now, that only after 50 years, only after a half century, there are the same trends, the same utmost fear in many countries starting with Germany, all over the world. And probably that's the same danger. So my message is never let this happen again. Educate yourself. Listen. Listen to-- to everybody. Learn. And especially, listen to people that went through this, that are really witnesses. Don't believe all the deniers, which-- which-- which are full of hatred full-- racist. Listen to people and-- and protect yourself and your children. And that-- that would be my message. Open-- open-- open up your mind. Open up your mind. The-- the-- the people have the same purposes in life. They are all equal. After-- you don't see the color or religion of the people. And it's very possible to live peacefully without hatred. And-- and just to see the example of what that brought, so that there are no winners. No winners. And when you see you go to places and you see the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] soil soaked with blood of innocent people. When you take battlefront, wars, they are a nuisance, too. Because also there are no winners. But at least there are two sides that are fighting. We were not any part to a fight. We live-- we mind our business. We lived our life. We were condemned to die, because we were Jews. Because we had a different shape of the noses or different shape of the ears. So that's why we were condemned to die. That was one-sided annihilation. That was a Holocaust which cannot be compared with-- with other tragedies, which are quite a few right now going in Europe and in Africa. But this is not the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any special message for your own grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: For my own grandchildren? Be happy. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Don't forget your family's past. Keep this. I am throwing to you. I am throwing a link to the bridge. Take it. And build on it. And try to implant to posterity to your children, maybe grandchildren. That's why I was so glad that, you know, Sherry was on the picture. So that will go maybe further than from my sons. She's already third generation. So that's what I-- I-- I wish them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I hope they-- that's, of course, up to them. We try to give them the best or the smartest what we thought is right for them. Of course, the rest is to them. They live in a [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] difficult world. We don't know. It's-- it's a danger of-- of-- I feel the danger of repetition of the same things. It's a lot of hatred. And I just hope that this generation, my grandchildren and yours, and everybody else's will never experience anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cut. And speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was able to recover some of the old pictures that friends sent to me after the war. Because on our departure from the ghetto to the Aryan side of Warsaw, we destroyed all the documents and all the pictures of the family we had. Because that could discriminate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was lucky to get my-- I was lucky to get my mother's picture from a friend. And that's an enlargement from a very small photo. I suppose that was probably 1930. I-- I am not quite-- no, wait. [INAUDIBLE] maybe in '32 or '33. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, that's a picture of my mother, the one in the middle. And this young-- this young girl, it's my sister. And that was taken in about 1935 or '36.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my husband's family. That was a wedding picture of his sister, of course, the bride. And this man to the right with glasses is his father. And that was in about 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, that's Henry and me. And that was Summer 1939, shortly before the war. It was in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Anytime.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so this is my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's my birth certificate and Christening. That was obtained from the church archives and the base to get the [NON-ENGLISH], which is a German identification card. That was an original certificate of a dead person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a marriage certificate. It's a fake. But we need to obtain this live together, to live on the assumed papers. So that was enough to-- to-- to-- to-- to get the [NON-ENGLISH] on the same name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anytime.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, that's me on my German identification card. It's called [NON-ENGLISH], which was an original document based on the birth certificate. And that's an original fingerprint and my signature. And that was essential for survival. We had to carry this with us all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, got it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the postcard that my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Again.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the postcard that my mother sent to her friend in Chicago from the Warsaw ghetto. And that-- that was December 1940. It's an original card with this-- with this German stamp.
